texts.label: text
texts.orig: Insu Jeong, Iqubal Hossain,* Asmaul Husna, and Tae-Hyun Kim*
texts.text: Insu Jeong, Iqubal Hossain,* Asmaul Husna, and Tae-Hyun Kim*

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Blending is a simple method through which one can effectively tailor new polymers exhibiting the properties of their parent ones. Because the original properties of polymers are maintained after blending, various studies have used these films as gas separation membranes. In this study, a new CO 2 separation membrane is developed by physically mixing a polymer of intrinsic microporosity (PIM) with high gas permeability, polyimide (PIM-PI), as the hard segment and CO 2 -philic PIM-poly(ethylene glycol)/poly(propylene glycol), or PIM-PEG/PPG, as the soft segment. Prepared by adding 5 mol.% of PIM-PEG/PPG to PIM-PI, the blended membrane PPB-5, with a tensile strength of 54 MPa and 35.5% elongation at break, shows better mechanical properties than commercial high-performance polymer membranes developed for gas separation, PEG-based blended membranes, and corresponding copolymer membranes with similar compositions developed in a previous study. In addition, it shows high CO 2 permeability (1552.6 Barrer) and CO2 /N 2 selectivity (29.3) due to the well-developed microphase separation characteristics originating from the optimal two-component composition, and the gas separation performance is close to the Robeson (2008) upper bound.

texts.orig: Carbon dioxide, CO 2 , which accounts for the largest portion of greenhouse gas emissions, is one of the main causes of global warming, resulting in abnormal climate and extreme weather events, such as ice melts, massive fires, heavy rain, and heat waves. [5-7] In an attempt to reduce the atmospheric CO 2 concentration, extensive research into carbon capture, utilization, and storage technology has been conducted to develop a set of methods for using various solutions to selectively capture and store CO 2 from fossil fuels and convert it into new materials, such as methanol and polymers. [6,8,9]

texts.orig: To this end, various methods for CO 2 separation have been proposed, including cryogenic distillation, solvent absorption, adsorption, and membrane separation. [10-12] Considerable research has been focused on gas separation using polymer membranes because this method is cheaper than other techniques, is easy to scale up, and allows for modularization. [ 13-20] However, these membranebased gas separation systems have low permselectivity compared to other methods. Thus, high-performance gas separation membranes with excellent gas permeability and thermomechanical properties must be developed to overcome these limitations. [ 12,19,20]

texts.orig: Permeability and selectivity are some of the most important parameters of membrane-based gas separation processes. Ideally, high-performance gas separation membranes achieve excellent gas permeability and selectivity simultaneously. In reality, however, the permeability and selectivity of membranes have a tradeoff relationship, and thus it is very difficult to improve them in parallel: one will always be compromised for the other. [ 21,22] This difficulty is evident in the Robeson plot, which represents the relationship between the permeability and selectivity of gas separation membranes. [23,24] The upper bound defines the highest achievable selectivity for a given permeability in this plot. Developing high-performance membranes beyond this upper bound is an active research area.

texts.orig: One of the most widely used CO 2 -philic rubbery polymers is poly(ethylene glycol), or PEG, due to strong quadrupolequadrupole moment interactions between CO 2 and the repeating ether groups contained in PEG. Therefore, CO2 has a high solubility in PEG. However, the high molecular weight of the PEG shows inherent crystallinity due to the ordering and packing structure, eventually leading to reduced permeability and other physical properties. [ 39-44] Poly(ethylene glycol)/poly(propylene glycol) (PEG/PPG) is a material in which PEG is combined with PPG, an isomer of PEG, to reduce the crystallinity of PEG for improved gas permeability. PEG/PPG has been widely used as an alternate material for gas separation membranes, [15,16,18,29,39,45] but it is difficult to cast these rubbery polymers into self-standing membranes due to their insufficient mechanical properties. Accordingly, these polymers have been developed mostly as copolymer-based, [29,44,46] crosslinked, [ 15,16,18,45,47] or blended polymer membranes [41-43,48-50] by adding rigid polymers.
texts.text: One of the most widely used CO 2 -philic rubbery polymers is poly(ethylene glycol), or PEG, due to strong quadrupolequadrupole moment interactions between CO 2 and the repeating ether groups contained in PEG. Therefore, CO2 has a high solubility in PEG. However, the high molecular weight of the PEG shows inherent crystallinity due to the ordering and packing structure, eventually leading to reduced permeability and other physical properties. [ 39-44] Poly(ethylene glycol)/poly(propylene glycol) (PEG/PPG) is a material in which PEG is combined with PPG, an isomer of PEG, to reduce the crystallinity of PEG for improved gas permeability. PEG/PPG has been widely used as an alternate material for gas separation membranes, [15,16,18,29,39,45] but it is difficult to cast these rubbery polymers into self-standing membranes due to their insufficient mechanical properties. Accordingly, these polymers have been developed mostly as copolymer-based, [29,44,46] crosslinked, [ 15,16,18,45,47] or blended polymer membranes [41-43,48-50] by adding rigid polymers.

texts.orig: Meanwhile, polymers with intrinsic microporosity, such as polyimide (PIM-PI), have recently garnered significant attention as highly permeable polymer materials for gas separation membranes. [29,30,52] These polymers have a distorted laddershaped structure that originates from the spiro structure of PIM, which inhibits the packing of the polymer backbone. As a result, PIM-PI-based membranes have high fractional free volume (FFV) and thus improved gas selectivity. These polymers also exhibit excellent thermodynamic stability and mechanical properties due to the effect of the PI. Despite all these advantages, the applicability of PIM-PI is limited because its gas selectivity is insufficient for use as high-performance separation membranes. [29,30]

texts.orig: In the present study, PIM-PEG/PPG and PIM-PI, two polymer components with different properties, were blended to fabricate membranes with high selectivity that retain the high permeability of PIM-PI and overcome the limitations of existing PIMPI-PEG/PPG copolymer-based membranes. With this approach, we expected that using PIM-PEG/PPG would minimize the contraction of the free volume and that the microphase separation between the two polymer components would contribute to improved gas separation properties (Figure 1b).
texts.text: In the present study, PIM-PEG/PPG and PIM-PI, two polymer components with different properties, were blended to fabricate membranes with high selectivity that retain the high permeability of PIM-PI and overcome the limitations of existing PIMPI-PEG/PPG copolymer-based membranes. With this approach, we expected that using PIM-PEG/PPG would minimize the contraction of the free volume and that the microphase separation between the two polymer components would contribute to improved gas separation properties (Figure 1b).

texts.orig: In addition, the PIM-PEG/PPG content was varied with respect to the PIM-PI content to determine the optimal mixing ratio of the two components; the contribution of PIM-PEG/PPG to improving the CO 2 selectivity was maximized while the high permeability of PIM-PI was retained. The effects of changes in the relative contents of PIM-PI and PIM-PEG/PPG on the thermal and mechanical properties, morphology, and gas permeability behavior of the resultant blended membranes were comprehensively examined.

texts.orig: PIM-dianhydride ( 4 ) was subjected to condensation polymerization with durene-diamine ( 5 ) and PEG/PPG-diamine ( 6 ) to obtain the two precursor polymers PIM-PI ( 2 ) and PIM-PEG/PPG ( 3 ), respectively, according to the procedure reported in the literature ( Scheme 1 ). [ 29,52]

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The structures of the obtained polymer components, PIM-PI and PIM-PEG/PPG, were analyzed using 1 HNMRspectroscopy (Figure S1, Supporting Information). In the case of PIM-PI ( 2 ), the integral ratio of the four aromatic hydrogen peaks, H a at 7.4 ppm, H d at 7.3 ppm, H b at 6.7 ppm, and H c at 6.4 ppm, was measured and found to be 2:2:2:2. In addition, the integral ratio of the aliphatic hydrogen peaks, i.e., Hg-Hh at 2.4-2.2 ppm, Hi at 2.0 ppm, and He -Hf at 1.4-1.2 ppm, was 4:12:12. The relative integral ratio of the hydrogen peaks observed in PIMdianhydride ( 4 ) and durene-diamine ( 5 ), that is, the integral ratio of the methyl peak (H i ) in durene and the aromatic hydrogen peaks (H a-d ), was ≈ 12:8. These results confirmed the successful synthesis of PIM-PI ( 2 ).

texts.orig: In the case of PIM-PEG/PPG ( 3 ), similar to PIM-PI, aromatic hydrogen peaks were observed at 7.5 ppm (H a ), 7.3 ppm (H d ), 6.8 ppm (H b ), and 6.5 ppm (H c ). In addition, an aliphatic hydrogen peak attributed to PEG was found at 4.0-3.5 ppm, and the characteristic hydrogen peaks of the PIM structure were observed at 2.5-2.0 ppm (H f , H g ) and 1.5-1.3 ppm (H e , H h ). These results confirmed the successful synthesis of PIM-PEG/PPG ( 3 ).

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.2. Fabrication of Blended Membranes, PPB-X
texts.text: 2.2. Fabrication of Blended Membranes, PPB-X

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The PPB-X, the blended membranes, was fabricated by blending PIM-PIandPIM-PEG/PPGwithmolarratios of PIM-PEG/PPG to PIM-PI of 1.5%, 3%, and 5% and subjecting the blends to membrane casting. When the PIM-PEG/PPG content exceeded 5 mol.%, membrane casting was impossible due to the reduced rigidity. Thus, in the present study, the PIM-PEG/PPG content was limited to 5 mol.%. All polymers were dissolved in chloroform and then processed into flexible membranes with a thickness of 40-50 µ m using solvent drying ( Figure 2 ). The blended membranes containing PIM-PI and PIM-PEG/PPG were named 'PPB-X', where the variable X refers to the molar ratio of PIM-PEG/PPG to PIM-PI.

When the PIM-PEG/PPG content exceeded 5 mol.%, membrane casting was impossible due to the reduced rigidity. Thus, in the present study, the PIM-PEG/PPG content was limited to 5 mol.%. All polymers were dissolved in chloroform and then processed into flexible membranes with a thickness of 40-50 µ m using solvent drying ( Figure 2 ). The blended membranes containing PIM-PI and PIM-PEG/PPG were named 'PPB-X', where the variable X refers to the molar ratio of PIM-PEG/PPG to PIM-PI.

texts.orig: The structures of the blended membranes were analyzed using FTIR spectroscopy. In all blended membranes, the characteristic peaks of PIM-PI were observed, regardless of the composition, as follows: at 645 cm -1 C-N (C -N out-of-plane bending), 744 cm -1 (imide ring bending), 1,097 cm -1 (C -O -C symmetric stretching), 1,356 cm -1 (imide C -N stretching), and 1,780 and 1,720 cm -1 (C = O imide asymmetric and symmetric stretching, respectively). A peak attributed to PIM-PEG/PPG (CH 2 -O -CH2 symmetric stretching at 944 cm -1 ) appeared in all PPB-X membranes. The intensity of this peak increased with increasing content of PIM-PEG/PPG. These results confirmed that PIMPEG/PPG had been successfully blended with PIM-PI (Figure S2, Supporting Information).

texts.orig: The physical properties of the blended membranes were analyzed ( Table 1 ). First, the density of the PPB-X membranes decreased with increasing PIM-PEG/PPG content: PPB-1.5 (1.335 g cm -3 ) > PPB-3 (1.269 g cm -3 ) > PPB-5 (1.183 g cm -3 ). This trend is attributed to the PEG/PPG component of PIMPEG/PPG, a rubbery polymer with a flexible structure and lower density than PIM-PI. Accordingly, increased PIM-PEG/PPG content led to a decrease in the density of PPB-X. [29,44,53]

texts.orig: Subsequently, the wide-angle X-ray diffraction (WAXD) spectra of the blended polymer membranes, together with that of the PIM-PI, were measured over a 2 /u1D703 range of 5 ° -30 ° . In all the PPB-X membranes, a broad peak was observed spanning the entire measurement range, indicating that the structures are non-crystalline. We also found that the 2 /u1D703 values at which the maximum intensity was observed increase in the order PIM-PI (11.26 ° ) < PPB-1.5 (11.44 ° ) < PPB-3 (11.56 ° ) < PPB-5 (11.71 ° ). The peak intensity shifts to higher scattering angles with increasing PIM-PEG/PPG content ( Figure 3 ). These measurements were then converted into d-spacing values using Bragg's law ( d = /u1D706 /2 sin /u1D703 , where /u1D706 is 1.789 Å and /u1D703 is the scattering angle). The d-spacing decreases slightly in the order PIM-PI (7.85 Å ) > PPB-1.5 (7.73 Å ) > PPB-3 (7.65 Å ) > PPB-5 (7.55 Å ), indicating that the spacing between the polymer chains gradually narrows with increasing PIM-PEG/PPG content. This is consistent with the trend observed in the FFV results above. Overall, the volume of the interchain space originating from PIM-PI decreases with increased PIM-PEG/PPG content.

texts.orig: Thethermal properties of the blended membranes were observed using thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), and the results were compared with those of the PIM-PI and PIM-PEG/PPG homopolymers ( Figure 4 a; Table S1, Supporting Information).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: PPB-X showed a two-step thermal degradation. In contrast, a single-step weight loss was observed in the two homopolymers (PIM-PI and PIM-PEG/PPG). The weight loss of PIM-PI was attributed to the decomposition of its polymer backbone. Using derivative thermogravimetry (DTG), the onset thermal decomposition temperature of PIM-PI ( T d2 ) was measured and found to be 497 ° C, and the maximum weight-loss temperature ( T max2 ) was measured and found to be 544 ° C; PIM-PI exhibited very high thermal stability (Figure 4b; Table S1, Supporting Information).
texts.text: PPB-X showed a two-step thermal degradation. In contrast, a single-step weight loss was observed in the two homopolymers (PIM-PI and PIM-PEG/PPG). The weight loss of PIM-PI was attributed to the decomposition of its polymer backbone. Using derivative thermogravimetry (DTG), the onset thermal decomposition temperature of PIM-PI ( T d2 ) was measured and found to be 497 ° C, and the maximum weight-loss temperature ( T max2 ) was measured and found to be 544 ° C; PIM-PI exhibited very high thermal stability (Figure 4b; Table S1, Supporting Information).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: PIM-PEG/PPG was less thermally stable than PIM-PI. The onset polymer backbone decomposition temperature ( T d1 ) was measured as 342 ° C (degradation of PEG/PPG), and the maximum weight-loss temperature ( T max1 ) was 408 ° C (degradation of PIM unit), indicating relatively high thermal stability.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: position temperature ( T d1 ), which was attributed to the decomposition of the soft PEG/PPG segment of PIM-PEG/PPG. The DTG results also confirmed that the rate of weight loss (%) increased with increased PIM-PEG/PPG content. The second weight loss at T d2 = 497 ° Cand T max2 = 544 ° Cwasattributed to the decomposition of the PIM-PI polymer backbone. These degradation temperatures were equivalent to those observed in the PIM-PI homopolymer. The weight-loss rate (%) corresponding to this peak, as well as the amount of residue at 800 ° C, decreased with increasing PIM-PEG/PPG content. Thus, the PPB-X membranes combined the thermal degradation behaviors of the two polymer components, PIM-PI and PIM-PEG/PPG, as blended membranes in which the two homopolymers were simply physically mixed. Based on these results, the PPB-X membranes developed in the present study demonstrated high thermal stability, making them suitable for use as gas separation membranes.

texts.orig: Table S1, Supporting Information). The results showed an increased amount of PIM-PEG/PPG. This elastic component decreased tensile strength and Young's modulus of PPB-X but increased the elongation at break. To be more specific, the tensile strength of the blended membranes gradually decreased in the order: pristine PIM-PI (66.9 MPa) > PPB-1.5 (62.2 MPa) > PPB3 (59.9 MPa) > PPB-5 (54.0 MPa). The Young's modulus also decreased in the same order: pristine PIM-PI (1.72 GPa) > PPB1.5 (1.33 GPa) > PPB-3 (1.3 GPa) > PPB-5(1.23 GPa). In contrast, the elongation at break increased with increased PIMPEG/PPG content, as follows: pristine PIM-PI (8%) < PPB-1.5 (24.6%) < PPB-3 (31.6%) < PPB-5 (35.5%).
texts.text: Table S1, Supporting Information). The results showed an increased amount of PIM-PEG/PPG. This elastic component decreased tensile strength and Young's modulus of PPB-X but increased the elongation at break. To be more specific, the tensile strength of the blended membranes gradually decreased in the order: pristine PIM-PI (66.9 MPa) > PPB-1.5 (62.2 MPa) > PPB3 (59.9 MPa) > PPB-5 (54.0 MPa). The Young's modulus also decreased in the same order: pristine PIM-PI (1.72 GPa) > PPB1.5 (1.33 GPa) > PPB-3 (1.3 GPa) > PPB-5(1.23 GPa). In contrast, the elongation at break increased with increased PIMPEG/PPG content, as follows: pristine PIM-PI (8%) < PPB-1.5 (24.6%) < PPB-3 (31.6%) < PPB-5 (35.5%).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The elongation at break of PPB-5 (35.5%), in particular, was ≈ 4.4 times that of pristine PIM-PI (8%). This was attributed to the elastic PIM-PEG/PPG intercalated into the free volume contained in the backbone of the rigid polymer PIM-PI. This resulted in a high degree of microphase separation between the polymer segments with different physical properties, allowing the resultant membranes to successfully combine the superior rigidity of PIM-PI with the high elasticity of PIM-PEG/PPG. As a result, excellent mechanical properties were achieved.

texts.orig: 2.6. Morphological Analysis
texts.text: 2.6. Morphological Analysis

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The morphological characteristics of the blended PPB-X membranes were analyzed using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM) ( Figure 5 ).
texts.text: The morphological characteristics of the blended PPB-X membranes were analyzed using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM) ( Figure 5 ).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The surface SEM images showed that, in all PPB-X membranes, there was a clear contrast between the dark, dense regions corresponding to PIM-PI and the bright regions corresponding to PIM-PEG/PPG. This was attributed to the phase separation between the hard (PIM-PI) and the soft (PIM-PEG/PPG) units. This phase separation became significantly more distinct with increasing PIM-PEG/PPG content (Figure 5a-c). The PIM-PEG/PPG was uniformly distributed throughout the matrix of each blended membrane. This further confirmed the successful blending of PIM-PEG/PPG with PIM-PI.

Furthermore, the measured oxygen content (atomic %) increased with increased PIM-PEG/PPG content, as follows: PPB1.5 (25.48%) < PPB-3 (26.03%) < PPB-5 (26.75%). (Figure S3a-c and Table S2, Supporting Information).

texts.orig: In our previous study, no significant changes in the morphology were observed in the copolymer membranes of PIM-PI and PIM-PEG/PPG until the PEG/PPG content reached > 20 mol.%. In the present study, however, only a small amount of added PIM-PEG/PPG, 1.5 mol.%, led to distinct phase separation. It is relatively difficult for the PIM-PEG/PPG polymer chains to be intercalated into the free space; specifically, the interchain space is made available by the backbone of PIM-PI, more so in blended membranes than in copolymer membranes, enhancing the degree of phase separation.

texts.orig: The gas separation performance of the PPB-X membranes was analyzed with respect to the PIM-PEG/PPG content by measuring the single gas permeability values of N 2 , CH4, and CO2 at 2 atm and 30 ° C. Subsequently, the results were compared with those of pristine PIM-PI, as well as the results of the PIM-(durene-PEG/PPG) random-type copolymer membranes reported in our previous study ( Table 2 ; Figure S5, Supporting Information). [29] In all studied polymer membranes, including pristine PIM-PI, the gas permeability decreased in the order P(CO2 ) > P(CH4) > P(N2 ). This trend is consistent with what is typically observed in most solubility-dominating PIM-type and soft polymer-based gas separation membranes.

More specifically, the permeability of N 2 , P(N 2 ), was measured and found to be 53 Barrer in PPB-5, an ≈ 64% reduction from that of PIM-PI, 148 Barrer. Similarly, the permeability of CH4, P(CH4 ), was 93.1 Barrer in PPB-5, an ≈ 55%reduction from that of PIM-PI, 209 Barrer. The permeability of CO 2 was ≈ 28% lower in PPB-5 compared to PIM-PI; P(CO 2 ) for PIM-PI and PPB-5were2,156 and 1,552.6 Barrer, respectively. The permeability reduction observed in the blended membranes was smaller for CO2 than the other gases studied. This is attributed to the smaller size of the CO 2 molecule and the increased dipole-quadruple interactions between ether oxygen and CO 2 . Furthermore, despite this permeability reduction, the selectivity of CO 2 increased with increased PIM-PEG/PPG content. Indeed, the CO 2 /N2 selectivity increased by ≈ 100%, from 14.6 (PIM-PI) to 29.3 (PPB-5). The CO2/CH4 selectivity increased by ≈ 62%, from 10.3 (PIM-PI) to 16.7 (PPB-5) (Table 2).

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.9. Anti-Aging Effect
texts.text: 2.9. Anti-Aging Effect

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the case of polymers with a PIM structure, the free volume in the polymer matrix decreases over time, which causes a decrease in the gas permeability and an increase in the selectivity of the corresponding membrane; this so-called 'aging' phenomenon limits the use of this highly permeable rigid polymer for gas separation applications. [ 29,30,53,54] The long-term durability of the blended membrane PPB-5, which displayed the best gas separation performance among the three studied PPB membranes, was investigated by measuring its CO 2 permeability and CO2/N2 selectivity for 300 days, and the results were compared with those of PIM-PI. The CO 2 permeability and selectivity were then normalized based on their initial values (Figure S6, Supporting Information); the results indicate that the CO 2 permeability of PIM-PI decreases by 15% and 22% after 3 and 7 days, respectively, while that of PPB-5 decreases by 12% and 16% after 3 and 7 days, respectively. After 300 days, PIM-PI shows a 50% decrease in permeability compared to its initial state, whereas PPB5 shows only a 40% decrease. In addition, the CO 2 /N2 selectivity increases more steeply in PIM-PI than PPB-5, indicating that the blended PPB-5 membrane experiences significantly less aging. We speculated that the insertion of PIM-PEG/PPG between the PIM-PI chains effectively inhibited the packing of the PIM-PI units, hence improving the 'anti-aging effect'.

texts.orig: 3. Conclusion
texts.text: 3. Conclusion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the present study, blended membranes with excellent CO 2 permeability and selectivity were fabricated by blending PIM-PI, which has high gas permeability, and PIM-PEG/PPG, which has high CO2 selectivity, at molar ratios of PIM-PEG/PPG:PIM-PI of 1.5%, 3%, and 5%, named PPB-1.5, PPB-3, and PPB-5, respectively. To retain the high permeability while minimizing the loss of mechanical properties, the PIM-PEG/PPG content was limited to 5 mol.%.

To retain the high permeability while minimizing the loss of mechanical properties, the PIM-PEG/PPG content was limited to 5 mol.%.

The corresponding blended membrane (PPB-5) exhibited excellent mechanical properties, with an elongation at break ≈ 4.4 times that of a non-blended pristine PIM-PI membrane, while maintaining its tensile strength at a similar level.
texts.text: When an appropriate amount (5 mol.%) of PIM-PEG/PPG was added, the degree of microphase separation between PIMPI and PIM-PEG/PPG, two polymer components with different physical properties, was maximized. The corresponding blended membrane (PPB-5) exhibited excellent mechanical properties, with an elongation at break ≈ 4.4 times that of a non-blended pristine PIM-PI membrane, while maintaining its tensile strength at a similar level.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In terms of morphological characteristics, the AFM and SEM results confirmed the presence of a distinct phase separation between PIM-PEG/PPG, a soft component, and PIM-PI, a hard component. The loss of the free volume of PIM-PI caused by the addition of PIM-PEG/PPG was not significant, limiting the reduction in the permeability of the blended membranes. The blended membranes developed in the present study exhibited excellent gas separation performance close to the 2008 upper bound for CO 2 /N2 of the Robeson plot. Notably, PPB-5, with the most distinct phase separation, exhibited the best CO 2 separation performance with P(CO 2 ) = 1,552.6 Barrer and CO 2 /N2 = 29.3. Furthermore, similar to the pure gas conditions, in mixed gas conditions, as the PIM-PEG/PPG content increased, the CO 2 permeability of the blended membranes decreased, but their CO 2 /N2 selectivity increased from 9.05 (PPB-1.5) to 10.10 (PPB-5).

texts.orig: In conclusion, the major findings of the present study confirm that high-performance membranes with excellent mechanical properties and superior gas permeability and selectivity can be obtained by blending PIM-PI (with high gas permeability) and PIM-PEG/PPG (with high CO2 selectivity) at an optimal mixing ratio. texts.orig: Synthesis of PIM-PI : Following the previous literature, a single-step synthesis was used to prepare the PIM-PI polymer 2 . The general procedure for the synthesis of PIM-PI is as follows: a mixture of an anhydride monomer ( 4 ) (600 mg and 95.45 × 10 -2 mmol), durene-diamine ( 5 ) (156.78 mg and 95.45 × 10 -2 mmol) in 7 mL of m-cresol was prepared in a dry round-bottomed flask (100 mL) equipped with a reflux condenser and a nitrogen inlet. The reaction mixture was stirred at room temperature (RT) for 30 min, and quinoline (60 µ L) and anhydrous toluene (1.3 mL) were added. The temperature was slowly increased to 200 ° C and then held constant for 12 h. The resulting highly viscous solution was cooled and diluted with toluene (5 mL) and then precipitated in vigorously stirred methanol (300 mL). The resulting solid precipitate was filtered and purified by re-precipitation from chloroform (5 mL) in methanol (400 mL), filtered, and finally dried in a vacuum oven at 80 ° C, resulting in pale yellow polymer beads (680.4 mg and 94.2%); /u1D6FF H (400 MHz, CDCl 3 ) 7.39 (2H, s, ArH), 7.30 (2H, s, ArH), 6.75 (2H, s, ArH), 6.42 (2H, s, ArH), 2.852.20 (4H, m, CH 2 ), 2.1-2.05 (12H, br signal, ArCH 3 ), 1.40-1.34 (12H, m, CCH3 ); ATR-FTIR (cm -1 ) 2,955, 2,922, and 2,862 (C-H asymmetric and symmetric stretching), 1,780 (imide C = O asymmetric stretching), 1,718 (imide C = Osymmetric stretching), 1,598, 1,480 (C = C), 1,354 (imide C = N stretching), 1,314, 1,190 (C-O-C asymmetric stretching) 1,108, (C-O-C symmetric stretching), 744 (imide ring deformation), and 645 (C-N outof-plane bending).

texts.orig: The corresponding polymers PIM-PI ( 2 ) and PIM-PEG/PPG ( 3 ) (1.5, 3, and 5 mol.%) were dissolved in CHCl 3 ( ≈ 3% w/v, g mL -1 ), stirred at RT overnight, and then filtered through plugs of cotton. For each sample, the solution was carefully poured into a Petri dish and covered with aluminum foil pierced with small holes. The solution underwent slow solvent evaporation at RT overnight. The sample in the Petri dish was then placed in an oven, and solvent evaporation was completed at 80 ° C for 12 h. After drying completely, the Petri dish was cooled to RT, the membrane was peeled from it, and dried again at 80 ° C for 24 h
